title: --group-add: no matching entries in group file
body:
### Issue Description

I'm running Podman as a rootless user named `containeruser`, and I want the user inside the container to match the host user (using `--userns=keep-id`). Additionally, I need the container user to be part of the `containeraccess` group, which also exists on the host.

However, I'm encountering the following issue when I try to add the group explicitly:

```sh
$ podman run --rm --userns=keep-id --group-add=containeraccess docker.io/alpine id
Error: looking up supplemental groups for container cb563138f5a29cf0cd721a413871202827b425172ed6c568a5fdd5b5e72cddc5: Unable to find group containeraccess: no matching entries in group file
```

If I use `--group-add=keep-groups` instead, I get:

```sh
uid=1001(containeruser) gid=1001(containeruser) groups=1001(containeruser),65534(nobody),1(bin)
```

So the container joins some of the host groups, but not containeraccess.

I'm using Podman 5.5.1 with the crun OCI runtime. How can I ensure the containeraccess group is available inside the container and that the container user is added to it?

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. sudo groupadd -g 165538 test
2. sudo usermod -aG test containeruser 
3. podman run --rm --userns=keep-id --group-add test docker.io/alpine id

### Describe the results you received

See Description

### Describe the results you expected

The user inside the container is part of the `containeraccess` group

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

None

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

```sh
$ cat /etc/subgid

dme:100000:65536
containeruser:165536:65536
```

### Additional information

Additional information like issue happens only occasionally or issue happens with a particular architecture or on a particular setting

comments.body:
`--group-add` adds groups defined inside the container and not the ones from the host. In fact it is immposible to add just one group from the host as those gids are not mapped in the user namespace.
`--group-add keep-groups` only works by skiping the setgroups, so you get all groups from that user leaked into contianer process but that still does not mean the gids are mapped, thus they always show up as nobody. However functionality wise they still work if you try to access something only owned by that group.
